---
title: "Project summary"
output: html_document
---

## What is this?

This project uses R to answer one policy question:

  "Does the government get good value for money from funding
   A-levels and apprenticeships?"

It does this using a method called cost-benefit analysis — the same
approach the UK government uses when deciding how to spend public money.

---

## What did I find?

For every £1 the government spends:

  - A-levels return £20.27 in economic value
  - Level 3 Apprenticeships return £15.62

Both are well above the break-even point of £1, which means both
pathways are worth funding. These results hold even when the
assumptions behind the numbers are challenged.

---

## How does the analysis work?

Think of it like a personal investment calculation — but for society.

COSTS
  The government pays to fund each student through their course.
  For A-levels that is around £9,800 over two years.
  For apprenticeships it is around £12,750 over 18 months.

BENEFITS
  The benefits flow back to society in four ways:

  1. Higher earnings for the student over their working life
  2. More income tax and National Insurance collected by the government
  3. Higher chance of being employed (less time out of work)
  4. Less spending on benefits

DISCOUNTING
  Money received in the future is worth less than money today —
  the same way you would prefer £100 now over £100 in 10 years.
  The analysis accounts for this using a 3.5% annual discount rate,
  as required by HM Treasury.

STRESS TESTING
  The results are re-run under different assumptions to check
  they are not just a product of one optimistic number. Both
  pathways still clear the break-even point across all scenarios.

---

## Why does this matter?

This kind of analysis directly informs Spending Review decisions —
when the government decides how much money to allocate to education,
evidence like this helps make the case for continued investment.

It is also an example of causal analysis, not just correlation.
The earnings figures come from research that tracks real people
over time and compares outcomes between those who took each
qualification and those who did not.

---

## Data used

All data is publicly available and free to use:

  - DfE / IFS Longitudinal Education Outcomes (LEO) — earnings data
  - DfE funding rates 2023/24 — cost data
  - HM Treasury Green Book (2026) — appraisal methodology
  - OECD Education at a Glance (2023) — international context
  - ONS Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings — earnings baseline
